exercises.title: Trace generic class instantiation
exercises.scenario: T is substituted with the actual type at compile time.
hints: s is Box<String>, so get() returns String
getSimpleName() of String = 'String'
n.get() returns Integer 42; 42 * 2 = 84

exercises.title: Declare generic class
exercises.scenario: Add type parameter to make a class generic.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make `Container` a generic class with type parameter T.

blanks.hint: Type parameter declaration after class name
Type parameter used as field type
Type parameter used as constructor param
Type parameter used as return type

hints: <T> after class name declares the type parameter
T is used as field, constructor param, and return type

exercises.title: Simple Box<T> class
exercises.scenario: Write and use the most basic generic class.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Box<T>` with get(), set(), and isEmpty(). Test with String and Integer.

Expected output:
String box: hello | empty: false
After clear: empty: true
Int box: 42

test_cases.expected_stdout:
String box: hello | empty: false
After clear: empty: true
Int box: 42

hints: class Box<T> { private T value; ... }
set(null) makes it empty, isEmpty() checks null

exercises.title: Pair<A, B> — two type parameters
exercises.scenario: Generic class with two different type parameters.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Pair<A, B>` with getFirst(), getSecond(), swap(), toString(). Test with String+Integer and String+Boolean.

Expected output:
(Hyderabad, 500032)
Swapped: (500032, Hyderabad)
(premium, true)
exercises.starter_code: // Pair<A,B> with two type parameters and swap()
hints: class Pair<A, B> — two separate type parameters
swap() returns Pair<B, A> — reversed types!
new Pair<>(second, first) — note: no explicit types needed, diamond infers

exercises.title: Generic Stack<T>
exercises.scenario: Classic stack data structure as a generic class.
exercises.instructions:
Implement `Stack<T>` with push(T), pop() (throws if empty), peek(), isEmpty(), size().

Test: push 3 ints, peek, pop all.

Expected output:
Size: 3, peek: 30
30
20
10
Empty: true
hints: Internally use List<T> for storage
pop/peek: remove/get last element
Throw NoSuchElementException when empty

exercises.title: Generic cache wrapper
exercises.scenario: Wrap a value with metadata in a generic class.
exercises.instructions:
Create `TimedEntry<T>` that stores a value and a timestamp (System.currentTimeMillis()). Methods: getValue(), getAge() (ms since creation), isExpired(long maxAgeMs).

Expected output:
Value: Aisha
Expired (0ms max): true
Expired (100000ms max): false
exercises.starter_code: // TimedEntry<T> with value, creation time, expiry check

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Value: Aisha
Expired (0ms max): true
Expired (100000ms max): false

hints: TimedEntry<T> — T is the value type
isExpired(0): any non-zero age > 0 → true
isExpired(100000): 100 seconds — entry is barely created → false

exercises.title: Triple<A, B, C> — three type parameters
exercises.scenario: Generic class with three type parameters.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Triple<A, B, C>` with three fields and getters. Add `toList()` that returns `List<Object>` with all three values. Add toString.

Expected output:
(Aisha, 28, Hyderabad)
[Aisha, 28, Hyderabad]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// Triple<A,B,C> with three type parameters
hints: class Triple<A, B, C> — three parameters
toList() returns List<Object> with Arrays.asList(first, second, third)

exercises.title: Generic Queue<T>
exercises.instructions:
Implement `Queue<T>` with enqueue(T), dequeue() → T, peek() → T, isEmpty(), size(). FIFO order.

Test: enqueue 3 strings, dequeue all.

Expected output:
Size: 3, front: Aisha
Aisha
Raj
Priya
Empty: true

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Size: 3, front: Aisha
Aisha
Raj
Priya
Empty: true

hints: LinkedList supports efficient addLast and removeFirst for FIFO
dequeue removes from front (FIFO order)
peek returns front without removing

exercises.title: ApiResponse<T> wrapper
exercises.scenario: Generic response wrapper — universal REST API pattern.
hints: Private constructor + factory methods
ok() and error() are static generic methods (Topic 9.2 preview)
T data is null for error responses

exercises.title: Type safety — catching errors at compile time
exercises.scenario: Generics prevent wrong-type usage at compile time.
exercises.instructions:
Which lines COMPILE and which cause COMPILE ERRORS? Answer C (compiles) or E (error) for each.

```java
Box<String> b = new Box<>("hello");   // Line 1
b.set(42);                             // Line 2
String s = b.get();                    // Line 3
Integer n = b.get();                   // Line 4
b.set(null);                           // Line 5
Box raw = new Box("oops");            // Line 6 — raw type (compiles with warning)
```
exercises.expected_solution:
Line 1: C
Line 2: E
Line 3: C
Line 4: E
Line 5: C
Line 6: C (with warning)

test_cases.expected:
Line 1: C
Line 2: E
Line 3: C
Line 4: E
Line 5: C
Line 6: C (with warning)

hints: Box<String> only accepts String values
Line 2: 42 is Integer, not String → E
Line 4: get() returns String, can't assign to Integer → E
Line 5: null is allowed for any reference type
Line 6: raw type — compiles with unchecked warning, not error

exercises.title: Generic wrapper with map
exercises.scenario: Generic class with transformation method (like Optional.map).
exercises.instructions:
Create `Wrapper<T>` with a value and a `map(Function<T, R>)` that returns `Wrapper<R>`. Also add `orElse(T)` for null-value case.

Test: Wrapper<String> mapped to Wrapper<Integer> (length), then Wrapper<String> (formatted).

Expected output:
Wrapper(hello)
Wrapper(5)
Wrapper(length=5)
null wrapper: no value
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// Wrapper<T> with map(Function) and orElse(T)

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Wrapper(hello)
Wrapper(5)
Wrapper(length=5)
null wrapper: no value

hints: map has its OWN type parameter <R> — independent of T
returns Wrapper<R> — new wrapper of different type
If value is null, return empty Wrapper<R>

exercises.title: Generic key-value store
exercises.scenario: Generic class managing type-safe key-value entries.
exercises.instructions:
Create `KeyValue<K, V>` with key (K), value (V), withValue(V newValue) that returns a new KeyValue with same key but new value, toString.

Test with (String, Integer) and (Integer, String) pairs.

Expected output:
userId=42
(updated) userId=99
rank=first
hints: class KeyValue<K, V> — two type parameters
withValue returns KeyValue<K,V> with same key, new value
immutable: creates new instance instead of mutating

exercises.title: Generic Result<T> — success/failure
exercises.scenario: The worked example extended with chaining.
exercises.instructions:
Build `Result<T>` (success/failure) with: success(T), failure(String), isSuccess(), getValue(), getError(), orElse(T), map(Function<T,R>). Chain three operations: parse string → double value → check positive.

Expected output:
Success(₹5000.0)
Failure(Not a number: five-hundred)
Failure(Must be positive)
Final: ₹5000.0
Final on failure: ₹0.0
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.function.*;
// Result<T> with success/failure and map chaining
exercises.note: This exercise is best left for students to implement from scratch; the expected output is what matters.
hints: Result<T> success holds value; failure holds error string
map: if success, apply function → new Result<R>; if failure, propagate
Chain: parseAmount('5000').map(fn).orElse(0.0)

exercises.title: Generic paginated list
exercises.scenario: Generic class for paginated data — real backend pattern.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Page<T>` representing one page of results:
- content: List<T>
- pageNumber: int (1-based)
- pageSize: int
- totalElements: long
- totalPages(): computed
- hasNext(): pageNumber < totalPages
- hasPrevious(): pageNumber > 1
- isEmpty(): content is empty

Test with a page of employees.

Expected output:
Page 2/4 (25 total)
Content: [Priya, Vikram, Anita]
Has next: true | Has prev: true
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// Page<T> — generic paginated result

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Page 2/4 (25 total)
Content: [Priya, Vikram, Anita]
Has next: true | Has prev: true

hints: totalPages = ceil(totalElements / pageSize)
25 elements, 7 per page → ceil(25/7) = ceil(3.57) = 4 pages
Page 2 of 4: has next (3,4) and has previous (1)

exercises.title: Generic event bus — producer/consumer pattern
exercises.scenario: Generic class for type-safe event dispatch.
exercises.instructions:
Create `EventBus<E>` (E = event type) that:
- Stores a list of `Consumer<E>` listeners
- `subscribe(Consumer<E>)` — add listener
- `publish(E event)` — notify all listeners
- `unsubscribeAll()` — clear listeners
- `subscriberCount()` — count

Test with String events and with a custom OrderEvent.

Expected output:
String events:
Listener1: Hello
Listener2: Hello
Listener1: World
Listener2: World
Order events:
Processing: ORD-001 (₹3500.0)
Logging: ORD-001

test_cases.expected_stdout:
String events:
Listener1: Hello
Listener2: Hello
Listener1: World
Listener2: World
Order events:
Processing: ORD-001 (₹3500.0)
Logging: ORD-001

hints: EventBus<E> stores List<Consumer<E>>
publish: listeners.forEach(l -> l.accept(event))
Works for any event type — same EventBus class, different E
